title: Analytical model for structured light propagation through a turbulent atmosphere
abstract: We develop a straightforward analytical framework for the propagation of spatial light modes through a turbulent atmosphere. Built upon the split-step approach with the mode-based optical field representation, it directly assesses how turbulence-induced phase fluctuations deplete the optical power in the original mode and re-distribute it into neighboring spatial modes. Importantly, this power transfer scales linearly with the propagation distance in a uniform channel, yielding a simple solution for arbitrary distances in the form of a matrix exponential. The transfer rate is determined by the spatial spectral overlap between the turbulence spectrum and the acceptance spectrum for a pair of interacting spatial modes. The model predicts the average power in each spatial mode and is exact when a single mode strongly dominates all others. Our predictions show reasonably good agreement with simulations up to medium-to-strong turbulence levels. The model also confirms the scalings with mode order previously known as empirical observations.
summary: This paper presents an analytical model for structured light propagation through atmospheric turbulence, treating the interaction as a mode-coupling problem. It derives the power evolution using a matrix exponential solution based on the spectral overlap between turbulence and mode acceptance. The framework is valuable for predicting modal crosstalk in free-space optical links under various turbulence strengths.
main_problem: The paper develops an analytical framework to model how structured light propagates through a turbulent atmosphere, specifically quantifying the power depletion from the original mode and its redistribution into neighboring spatial modes.
main_result: The power transfer rate scales linearly with propagation distance in a uniform channel, allowing for an exact solution via a matrix exponential. The model confirms scaling laws for mode coupling based on the mode order.
method: The authors employ a split-step approach combined with a mode-based optical field representation, treating the channel effect as a cumulative action of infinitesimal phase screens.
model_or_system: The system is free-space optical communication through a turbulent atmosphere. The model uses orthogonal mode sets (HG and LG) and derives coupling rates ($\Lambda_{ab}$) from the spatial spectral overlap between the turbulence spectrum and the mode acceptance spectrum.
key_observables: Average power in each spatial mode, power transfer rate, and the Rytov parameter ($\sigma_R^2$).
important_parameters: Propagation distance ($L$), Fried parameter ($r_0$), and the turbulence spectrum (e.g., Kolmogorov scaling).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the neglect of relative phases between spatial modes and is most accurate for weak to moderate turbulence levels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the physical setup and the split-step representation, showing power distribution comparisons between theoretical predictions and numerical simulations for various turbulence strengths.
paper_structure: The paper builds the model using the split-step approach, derives the mode coupling matrix ($\Lambda_{ab}$) based on phase perturbation spectra, and presents the final evolution as a matrix exponential solution, validating results against simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, analytically tractable tool for understanding modal cross-talk and energy redistribution in complex wave propagation environments, which has parallels in open quantum systems where environmental coupling leads to mode mixing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Key elements of the proposed framework. a. Schematic of the implied physical setup: a single spa- tial mode of light enters an extended channel with a turbu- lent medium, where it gets distorted. b. The same problem in the split-step representation: the channel is divided into a large number of random and independent chunks. Each chunk is modeled as a zero-thickness phase screen (PSi) and a free propagation region defined by the propagator ˆP. The resulting field in the end of the channel is decomposed into spatial modes to analyze their statistics. Our key proposal is to use the depicted optical-power-versus-spatial-mode encod- ing, which turns ˆP into the identity operator;...

FIG. 2. Distribution of power among mode groups as a func- tion of N for 3 channels with fundamental-mode input and characterized by interaction strengths |Λ00L| = 0.1, 1.0, and 3.0, which is equivalent to Fried parameter values of 204 mm, 51.3 mm, and 26.6 mm respectively. Based on 120 lowest- order LG modes. The total power in these modes is 0.996, 0.95, and 0.8 respectively, while the rest is dissipated to even higher order modes.

FIG. 3. Distribution of power among HG modes in an ex- tended channel with the length of L = 5900 m and the inter- action strength |Λ00L| = 3.0. The Rytov parameter σ2 R = 3.2. Theoretical prediction, which does not depend on the channel length unless the beam diameter varies, is based on 28 lowest- order HG modes. Modes are presented in the following order: (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (0,2), (1,1), (2,0), etc.
